theorems.title: General VC-dimension sample lower bound
theorems.proofNote: The proof contains the separately numbered Lemmas 2 and 3; the complete composite proof is preserved here, and both lemmas are also included separately.
theorems.statementHtml:
Assume \(0<\epsilon\leq\tfrac18\), \(0<\delta\leq\tfrac1{100}\), and \(\operatorname{VCdim}(C)\geq2\). Then any \((\epsilon,\delta)\)-learning algorithm \(A\) for \(C\) must use sample size

\[m_A(\epsilon,\delta)\geq\frac{\operatorname{VCdim}(C)-1}{32\epsilon}=\Omega\!\left(\frac{\operatorname{VCdim}(C)}{\epsilon}\right).\]
theorems.proofHtml:
Let the set \(X_0=\{x_0,x_1,\ldots,x_d\}\subseteq X\) be shattered by \(C\), where \(d=\operatorname{VCdim}(C)-1\). We define the following distribution \(P\) on \(X\):

\[P(x_0)=1-8\epsilon,\qquad P(x_i)=\frac{8\epsilon}{d},\quad 1\leq i\leq d.\]

Since \(P\) is 0 except on \(X_0\), we may assume without loss of generality that \(X=X_0\) and \(C\subseteq2^{X_0}\). Since \(X_0\) is shattered by \(C\), we may further assume that \(C=2^{X_0}\). Let \(C_0\subseteq C\) be defined by

\[C_0=\{\{x_0\}\cup T:T\subseteq\{x_1,\ldots,x_d\}\}.\]

Fix \(\epsilon\leq\tfrac18\) and \(\delta\leq\tfrac1{100}\), and let \(m=m_A(\epsilon,\delta)\). We define the set \(S\subset X^m\) to be the set of all \(\vec x\in X^m\) such that there are at most \(d/2\) distinct elements from \(\{x_1,\ldots,x_d\}\) appearing in \(\vec x\).

Lemma 2. Let \(m=m_A(\epsilon,\delta)\), \(k=r_A(\epsilon,\delta)\), and let \(U\) be the uniform distribution on \(\{0,1\}\). Then there exists a \(c_0\in C_0\) satisfying

\[\Pr_{\vec x\in P^m,r\in U^k}\!\left(e_A(\langle\vec x,c_0\rangle,r)>\epsilon\right)>\frac17P^m(S).\]

Proof. Fix \(\vec x_0\in S\) and the bit string \(r_0\in\{0,1\}^k\), and let \(l\) be the number of distinct elements of \(\{x_1,\ldots,x_d\}\) appearing in \(\vec x_0\). Note that \(l\leq d/2\) by definition of \(S\). Now for each \(c\in C_0\), there are exactly \(2^{d-l}\) concepts in \(C_0\) that are consistent with the sample \(\langle\vec x_0,c\rangle\), since \(\{x_1,\ldots,x_d\}\) is shattered by \(C_0\). For any of the \(d-l\) points in \(\{x_1,\ldots,x_d\}\) that do not appear in \(\vec x_0\), half of these concepts will contain the point, and half will not, hence \(h_A(\langle\vec x_0,c\rangle,r)\) is correct on this point for exactly half of these \(2^{d-l}\) consistent concepts. Thus, if we denote by \(w_A(\langle\vec x,c\rangle,r)\) the number of points in \(\{x_1,\ldots,x_d\}\) on which \(h_A(\langle\vec x,c\rangle,r)\) and \(c\) disagree, and by \(Q\) the uniform distribution over \(C_0\), then we have shown (for \(\vec x_0\) and \(r_0\) fixed)

\[\mathbb E_{c\in Q}\!\left(w_A(\langle\vec x_0,c\rangle,r_0)\right)\geq\frac{d-l}{2}\geq\frac d4.\]

The first inequality comes from the fact that the hypothesis of \(A\) may also be incorrect on points that did appear in \(\vec x_0\), and the second inequality from the fact that \(l\leq d/2\). Without loss of generality we may assume that any learning algorithm is always correct on the point \(x_0\) whenever concepts are selected from \(C_0\), so we may write

\[e_A(\langle\vec x_0,c\rangle,r_0)=\frac{8\epsilon}{d}w_A(\langle\vec x_0,c\rangle,r_0).\]

Then

\[\begin{aligned}\mathbb E_{c\in Q}\!\left(e_A(\langle\vec x_0,c\rangle,r_0)\right)&=\mathbb E_{c\in Q}\!\left(\frac{8\epsilon}{d}w_A(\langle\vec x_0,c\rangle,r_0)\right)\\&=\frac{8\epsilon}{d}\mathbb E_{c\in Q}\!\left(w_A(\langle\vec x_0,c\rangle,r_0)\right)\geq\frac{8\epsilon}{d}\frac d4=2\epsilon.\end{aligned}\tag{1}\]

Let \(P^m\mid S\) be the distribution \(P^m\) restricted to the set \(S\). Since (1) holds for any fixed \(\vec x_0\in S\) and any fixed bit string \(r_0\), if we instead choose \(\vec x\) randomly according to \(P^m\mid S\) and \(r\) randomly according to \(U^k\), we have

\[\mathbb E_{c\in Q,\vec x\in P^m\mid S,r\in U^k}\!\left(e_A(\langle\vec x,c\rangle,r)\right)\geq2\epsilon.\]

Thus, there must be some fixed \(c_0\in C_0\) satisfying

\[\tag{2}\mathbb E_{\vec x\in P^m\mid S,r\in U^k}\!\left(e_A(\langle\vec x,c_0\rangle,r)\right)\geq2\epsilon.\]

On the other hand, we have

\[\tag{3}e_A(\langle\vec x,c_0\rangle,r)\leq8\epsilon\]

for any \(\vec x\in X^m\) and any bit string \(r\), since we assume that the hypothesis of \(A\) is correct on the point \(x_0\). From (2) and (3) we can show

\[\tag{4}\Pr_{\vec x\in P^m\mid S,r\in U^k}\!\left(e_A(\langle\vec x,c_0\rangle,r)\geq\epsilon\right)>\frac17.\]

To see this, let \(\psi\) be a random variable whose expectation \(\mathbb E(\psi)\) according to some distribution is at least \(2\epsilon\), but whose absolute value is bounded above by \(8\epsilon\) (as is the case with the random variable \(e_A(\langle\vec x,c_0\rangle,r)\)). Then if \(p\) is the probability that \(\psi\) is larger than \(\epsilon\), we have

\[2\epsilon\leq\mathbb E(\psi)<p8\epsilon+(1-p)\epsilon.\]

Solving for \(p\), we obtain \(p>1/7\) as claimed.

Now from (4) we have

\[\Pr_{\vec x\in P^m,r\in U^k}\!\left(e_A(\langle\vec x,c_0\rangle,r)\geq\epsilon\right)\geq P^m(S)\Pr_{\vec x\in P^m\mid S,r\in U^k}\!\left(e_A(\langle\vec x,c_0\rangle,r)\geq\epsilon\right)>\frac17P^m(S).\]

This completes the proof of the lemma.

Lemma 3. Let \(\delta\leq\tfrac1{100}\). For \(m=d/(32\epsilon)\), \(P^m(S)>7\delta\).

Proof. We will use the following fact from probability theory (Proposition 2.4, [AV79]): For \(0\leq p\leq1\) and \(m,r\) positive integers, let \(GE(p,m,r)\) denote the probability of at least \(r\) successes in \(m\) independent trials of a Bernoulli variable with probability of success \(p\).

Fact. For any \(0\leq\alpha\leq1\), \(GE(p,m,(1+\alpha)mp)\leq e^{-\alpha^2mp/3}\).

Let \(E\) be the event that a point drawn at random according to \(P\) is contained in \(\{x_1,\ldots,x_d\}\). Thus, \(P(E)=8\epsilon\). Now if \(\vec x\) is drawn from \(P^m\) and \(\vec x\notin S\), then clearly event \(E\) must occur at least \(d/2\) times in \(\vec x\). The probability of event \(E\) occurring at least \(d/2\) times in \(m\) draws from \(P\) is bounded above by \(GE(8\epsilon,m,d/2)\). Setting \(m=d/(32\epsilon)\), we have

\[GE\!\left(8\epsilon,\frac{d}{32\epsilon},\frac d2\right)=GE\!\left(8\epsilon,\frac{d}{32\epsilon},2\frac{d}{32\epsilon}8\epsilon\right)\leq e^{-d/12}\leq e^{-1/12}.\]

But \(e^{-1/12}<1-7\delta\) for \(\delta<1/100\). Thus, for such \(\delta\) we have \(P^m(S)>7\delta\).

By Lemmas 2 and 3, if \(m_A(\epsilon,\delta)\leq d/(32\epsilon)\), then there is probability at least \(\delta\) that \(A\) outputs a hypothesis with error greater than \(\epsilon\), thus proving the theorem.

theorems.title: A hard target concept on low-coverage samples
theorems.statementHtml:
Let \(m=m_A(\epsilon,\delta)\), \(k=r_A(\epsilon,\delta)\), and let \(U\) be the uniform distribution on \(\{0,1\}\). Then there exists a \(c_0\in C_0\) satisfying

\[\Pr_{\vec x\in P^m,r\in U^k}\!\left(e_A(\langle\vec x,c_0\rangle,r)>\epsilon\right)>\frac17P^m(S).\]
theorems.proofHtml:
Fix \(\vec x_0\in S\) and the bit string \(r_0\in\{0,1\}^k\), and let \(l\) be the number of distinct elements of \(\{x_1,\ldots,x_d\}\) appearing in \(\vec x_0\). Note that \(l\leq d/2\) by definition of \(S\). Now for each \(c\in C_0\), there are exactly \(2^{d-l}\) concepts in \(C_0\) that are consistent with the sample \(\langle\vec x_0,c\rangle\), since \(\{x_1,\ldots,x_d\}\) is shattered by \(C_0\). For any of the \(d-l\) points in \(\{x_1,\ldots,x_d\}\) that do not appear in \(\vec x_0\), half of these concepts will contain the point, and half will not, hence \(h_A(\langle\vec x_0,c\rangle,r)\) is correct on this point for exactly half of these \(2^{d-l}\) consistent concepts. Thus, if we denote by \(w_A(\langle\vec x,c\rangle,r)\) the number of points in \(\{x_1,\ldots,x_d\}\) on which \(h_A(\langle\vec x,c\rangle,r)\) and \(c\) disagree, and by \(Q\) the uniform distribution over \(C_0\), then we have shown (for \(\vec x_0\) and \(r_0\) fixed)

\[\mathbb E_{c\in Q}\!\left(w_A(\langle\vec x_0,c\rangle,r_0)\right)\geq\frac{d-l}{2}\geq\frac d4.\]

The first inequality comes from the fact that the hypothesis of \(A\) may also be incorrect on points that did appear in \(\vec x_0\), and the second inequality from the fact that \(l\leq d/2\). Without loss of generality we may assume that any learning algorithm is always correct on the point \(x_0\) whenever concepts are selected from \(C_0\), so we may write

\[e_A(\langle\vec x_0,c\rangle,r_0)=\frac{8\epsilon}{d}w_A(\langle\vec x_0,c\rangle,r_0).\]

Then

\[\begin{aligned}\mathbb E_{c\in Q}\!\left(e_A(\langle\vec x_0,c\rangle,r_0)\right)&=\mathbb E_{c\in Q}\!\left(\frac{8\epsilon}{d}w_A(\langle\vec x_0,c\rangle,r_0)\right)\\&=\frac{8\epsilon}{d}\mathbb E_{c\in Q}\!\left(w_A(\langle\vec x_0,c\rangle,r_0)\right)\geq\frac{8\epsilon}{d}\frac d4=2\epsilon.\end{aligned}\tag{1}\]

Let \(P^m\mid S\) be the distribution \(P^m\) restricted to the set \(S\). Since (1) holds for any fixed \(\vec x_0\in S\) and any fixed bit string \(r_0\), if we instead choose \(\vec x\) randomly according to \(P^m\mid S\) and \(r\) randomly according to \(U^k\), we have

\[\mathbb E_{c\in Q,\vec x\in P^m\mid S,r\in U^k}\!\left(e_A(\langle\vec x,c\rangle,r)\right)\geq2\epsilon.\]

Thus, there must be some fixed \(c_0\in C_0\) satisfying

\[\tag{2}\mathbb E_{\vec x\in P^m\mid S,r\in U^k}\!\left(e_A(\langle\vec x,c_0\rangle,r)\right)\geq2\epsilon.\]

On the other hand, we have

\[\tag{3}e_A(\langle\vec x,c_0\rangle,r)\leq8\epsilon\]

for any \(\vec x\in X^m\) and any bit string \(r\), since we assume that the hypothesis of \(A\) is correct on the point \(x_0\). From (2) and (3) we can show

\[\tag{4}\Pr_{\vec x\in P^m\mid S,r\in U^k}\!\left(e_A(\langle\vec x,c_0\rangle,r)\geq\epsilon\right)>\frac17.\]

To see this, let \(\psi\) be a random variable whose expectation \(\mathbb E(\psi)\) according to some distribution is at least \(2\epsilon\), but whose absolute value is bounded above by \(8\epsilon\) (as is the case with the random variable \(e_A(\langle\vec x,c_0\rangle,r)\)). Then if \(p\) is the probability that \(\psi\) is larger than \(\epsilon\), we have

\[2\epsilon\leq\mathbb E(\psi)<p8\epsilon+(1-p)\epsilon.\]

Solving for \(p\), we obtain \(p>1/7\) as claimed.

Now from (4) we have

\[\Pr_{\vec x\in P^m,r\in U^k}\!\left(e_A(\langle\vec x,c_0\rangle,r)\geq\epsilon\right)\geq P^m(S)\Pr_{\vec x\in P^m\mid S,r\in U^k}\!\left(e_A(\langle\vec x,c_0\rangle,r)\geq\epsilon\right)>\frac17P^m(S).\]

This completes the proof of the lemma.

theorems.title: Low-coverage samples have sufficient probability
theorems.proofHtml:
We will use the following fact from probability theory (Proposition 2.4, [AV79]): For \(0\leq p\leq1\) and \(m,r\) positive integers, let \(GE(p,m,r)\) denote the probability of at least \(r\) successes in \(m\) independent trials of a Bernoulli variable with probability of success \(p\).

Fact. For any \(0\leq\alpha\leq1\), \(GE(p,m,(1+\alpha)mp)\leq e^{-\alpha^2mp/3}\).

Let \(E\) be the event that a point drawn at random according to \(P\) is contained in \(\{x_1,\ldots,x_d\}\). Thus, \(P(E)=8\epsilon\). Now if \(\vec x\) is drawn from \(P^m\) and \(\vec x\notin S\), then clearly event \(E\) must occur at least \(d/2\) times in \(\vec x\). The probability of event \(E\) occurring at least \(d/2\) times in \(m\) draws from \(P\) is bounded above by \(GE(8\epsilon,m,d/2)\). Setting \(m=d/(32\epsilon)\), we have

\[GE\!\left(8\epsilon,\frac{d}{32\epsilon},\frac d2\right)=GE\!\left(8\epsilon,\frac{d}{32\epsilon},2\frac{d}{32\epsilon}8\epsilon\right)\leq e^{-d/12}\leq e^{-1/12}.\]

But \(e^{-1/12}<1-7\delta\) for \(\delta<1/100\). Thus, for such \(\delta\) we have \(P^m(S)>7\delta\).

theorems.title: Previous general sample lower bound
theorems.proofNote: The paper states this result without proof, citing [BEHW86,87b].
theorems.statementHtml:
[BEHW86,87b] Let \(C\) be a non-trivial concept class. Then any \((\epsilon,\delta)\)-learning algorithm \(A\) for \(C\) must use sample size

\[m_A(\epsilon,\delta)=\Omega\!\left(\frac1\epsilon\ln\frac1\delta+\operatorname{VCdim}(C)\right).\]

theorems.title: Combined optimal-order lower bound
theorems.statementHtml:
Let \(C\) be a non-trivial concept class. Then any \((\epsilon,\delta)\)-learning algorithm \(A\) for \(C\) must use sample size

\[m_A(\epsilon,\delta)=\Omega\!\left(\frac1\epsilon\ln\frac1\delta+\frac{\operatorname{VCdim}(C)}{\epsilon}\right).\]
theorems.proofHtml: Theorem 1 improves separate lower bounds proportional to \(\operatorname{VCdim}(C)\) and \(1/\epsilon\) to a single lower bound that is proportional to their product. Using Theorems 1 and 4, we obtain:

theorems.title: Sample bounds for consistent algorithms
theorems.proofNote: The paper states this result without proof, citing [V82] and [BEHW86,87a,87b].
theorems.statementHtml:
[V82] [BEHW86,87a,87b] Let \(A\) be a consistent algorithm for \(C\) using hypothesis space \(C\), and let \(0<\epsilon,\delta<1\). Then

  1. \(A\) is an \((\epsilon,\delta)\)-learning algorithm for \(C\) with sample size \[m_A(\epsilon,\delta)=O\!\left(\frac1\epsilon\ln\frac1\delta+\frac{\operatorname{VCdim}(C)}{\epsilon}\ln\frac1\epsilon\right).\]
  2. If \(C\) is finite then \(A\) is an \((\epsilon,\delta)\)-learning algorithm for \(C\) with sample size \[m_A(\epsilon,\delta)=O\!\left(\frac1\epsilon\ln\frac1\delta+\frac{\ln|C|}{\epsilon}\right).\]

theorems.title: VC dimension of k-decision lists
theorems.statementHtml: Let \(k\mathrm{DL}\) be the class of \(k\)-Decision Lists. Then \(\operatorname{VCdim}(k\mathrm{DL})=\Theta(n^k)\).
theorems.proofHtml:
The lower bound on the dimension follows easily from the fact that \(k\mathrm{DL}\) contains the class of \(k\mathrm{DNF}\) functions [R87], thus \(\operatorname{VCdim}(k\mathrm{DL})\geq\operatorname{VCdim}(k\mathrm{DNF})=\Theta(n^k)\). For the upper bound, we begin by proving that \(\operatorname{VCdim}(1\mathrm{DL})=O(n)\). We then give a simple transformation that proves the theorem for arbitrary \(k\). We adopt the following notation: let

\[L=\langle(l_1,b_1),(l_2,b_2),\ldots,(l_m,b_m)\rangle\]

denote a 1-Decision List, where each \(l_i\) is a literal over the Boolean variables \(\{x_1,\ldots,x_n\}\), and each \(b_i\in\{0,1\}\). We will call each pair \((l_i,b_i)\) an item of the list \(L\).

We show that the class of \(1\mathrm{DL}\) functions is linearly separable—that is, for each \(L\in1\mathrm{DL}\) there is a linear function

\[P_L(x_1,\ldots,x_n)=c_1x_1+c_2x_2+\cdots+c_nx_n\]

with coefficients \(c_i\in\mathbb R\), \(1\leq i\leq n\) and a threshold value \(\tau\in\mathbb R\) such that for \(x_i\in\{0,1\}\), \(1\leq i\leq n\)

\[\tag{1}P_L(x_1,\ldots,x_n)\geq\tau\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad L(x_1,\ldots,x_n)=1.\]

Let \(C\) be the class of all half-spaces in Euclidean \(n\)-dimensional space. Then it follows that \(\operatorname{VCdim}(1\mathrm{DL})\leq\operatorname{VCdim}(C)=n+1\) (see e.g., [WD81], [HW87]). Hence \(\operatorname{VCdim}(1\mathrm{DL})=O(n)\).

\(P_L\) is constructed as follows: with each item \((l_i,b_i)\), associate a linear term \(T_i=T(l_i,b_i)\) involving a variable in \(\{x_1,\ldots,x_n\}\), defined by

\[T(x_i,1)=x_i\qquad T(x_i,0)=1-x_i\qquad T(\overline{x_i},1)=1-x_i\qquad T(\overline{x_i},0)=x_i.\]

We now describe a method for constructing a linear inequality of the form

\[\tag{2}P_L(x_1,\ldots,x_n)=a_1T_1+\cdots+a_mT_m\geq\tau\]

with \(a_i\in\mathbb R\), \(1\leq i\leq m\), and satisfying (1). This is done by constructing the coefficients \(a_i\) and \(\tau\) in the order \(a_m,\ldots,a_1,\tau\) according to the following inductive rules. We assume without loss of generality that \(b_m=1\); if \(b_m=0\) then \(L\) is equivalent to the list of length \(m-1\) obtained by deleting the \(m\)th item.

  1. (First step) \(a_m=1\).
  2. (The 0-Rule) If \(b_i=0\) then \(a_i=1+\sum_{j=i+1}^{m}a_j\).
  3. (The 1-Rule) If \(b_i=1\) then \(a_i=\sum_{j=i+1}^{k}a_j\), where \(k\) is the least \(l\), \(i+1\leq l\leq m\) such that \(b_l=1\).
  4. (The \(\tau\)-Rule) \(\tau=\sum_{j=1}^{k}a_j\), where \(k\) is the least \(l\), \(1\leq l\leq m\) such that \(b_l=1\).

Note that this procedure always constructs non-negative coefficients for the linear terms \(T_i\), so \(P_L(x_1,\ldots,x_n)\geq0\) always. We also note that \(\tau\geq0\) and \(P_L(x_1,\ldots,x_n)\leq\sum_{j=1}^{m}a_j\) for \(0\leq x_i\leq1\).

We now show by induction on the length \(m\) of \(L\) that the inequality (2) constructed by the above method satisfies (1).

Base Cases: Let \(m=1\). Then either \(L=\langle(x_i,1)\rangle\) or \(L=\langle(\overline{x_i},1)\rangle\). The linear inequalities constructed for these two cases by the above rules are \(x_i\geq1\) and \(1-x_i\geq1\), respectively, and these inequalities clearly satisfy (1).

Inductive Step: Let \(L'=\langle(l_2,b_2),\ldots,(l_m,b_m)\rangle\)—i.e., \(L'\) is simply \(L\) with the first item deleted. Then by the inductive hypothesis, there is a linear function \(P_{L'}(x_1,\ldots,x_n)\) and a threshold \(\tau'\) constructed by the above rules and satisfying

\[\tag{3}P_{L'}(x_1,\ldots,x_n)\geq\tau'\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad L'(x_1,\ldots,x_n)=1.\]

Consider the following cases:

Case 1. Suppose \((l_1,b_1)=(x_i,1)\). Then by application of the 1-Rule and the \(\tau\)-Rule, we obtain the linear inequality

\[P_L(x_1,\ldots,x_n)=\tau'x_i+P_{L'}(x_1,\ldots,x_n)\geq\tau'.\]

Now suppose that \(L(x_1,\ldots,x_n)=1\). Then either \(x_i=1\), or \(x_i=0\) and \(L'(x_1,\ldots,x_n)=1\). If \(x_i=1\) then \(P_L(x_1,\ldots,x_n)\geq\tau'\) since \(P_{L'}(x_1,\ldots,x_n)\geq0\). If \(L'(x_1,\ldots,x_n)=1\), then \(P_L(x_1,\ldots,x_n)\geq\tau'\) since \(P_{L'}(x_1,\ldots,x_n)\geq\tau'\) by (3). On the other hand, if \(L(x_1,\ldots,x_n)=0\) then \(x_i=0\) and \(P_{L'}(x_1,\ldots,x_n)<\tau'\) by (3), hence \(P_L(x_1,\ldots,x_n)<\tau'\).

Case 2. Suppose \((l_1,b_1)=(\overline{x_i},1)\) Then by application of the 1-Rule and the \(\tau\)-Rule, we obtain

\[P_L(x_1,\ldots,x_n)=\tau'(1-x_i)+P_{L'}(x_1,\ldots,x_n)\geq\tau'\]

and the analysis is similar to that of Case 1.

Case 3. Suppose \((l_1,b_1)=(x_i,0)\). Then by application of the 0-Rule and the \(\tau\)-Rule, we obtain the inequality

\[\tag{4}P_L(x_1,\ldots,x_n)=\left(1+\sum_{j=2}^{m}a_j\right)(1-x_i)+P_{L'}(x_1,\ldots,x_n)\geq\left(1+\sum_{j=2}^{m}a_j\right)+\tau'.\]

Now if \(L(x_1,\ldots,x_n)=1\) then we must have \(x_i=0\) and \(L'(x_1,\ldots,x_n)=1\). Hence using (3) we see that (4) is satisfied. Conversely, suppose that (4) is satisfied. Then \(x_i=0\) since \(P_{L'}(x_1,\ldots,x_n)\leq\sum_{j=2}^{m}a_j\) and \(\tau'\geq0\) always. Hence \(P_{L'}(x_1,\ldots,x_n)\geq\tau'\), so \(L'(x_1,\ldots,x_n)=1\) by (3), and thus \(L(x_1,\ldots,x_n)=1\).

Case 4. Suppose \((l_1,b_1)=(\overline{x_i},0)\). Then by the 0-Rule and the \(\tau\)-Rule, we obtain

\[P_L(x_1,\ldots,x_n)=\left(1+\sum_{j=2}^{m}a_j\right)x_i+P_{L'}(x_1,\ldots,x_n)\geq\left(1+\sum_{j=2}^{m}a_j\right)+\tau'\]

and the analysis is similar to Case 3.

Thus, 1-Decision Lists are linearly separable, and \(\operatorname{VCdim}(1\mathrm{DL})=O(n)\).

To see that \(\operatorname{VCdim}(k\mathrm{DL})=O(n^k)\), we use the following transformation: For each of the \(p(n)=\Theta(n^k)\) monomials \(M_i\) of length at most \(k\), create a variable \(y_i(x_1,\ldots,x_n)\) defined by

\[y_i(x_1,\ldots,x_n)=1\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad M_i(x_1,\ldots,x_n)=1.\]

Then under this transformation, each monomial \(M_i\) is mapped to a variable \(y_i\) in \(\Theta(n^k)\) dimensions, so a \(k\)-Decision List \(L\) is mapped to a 1-Decision List \(\widetilde L\) in the variables \(\{y_i\}\). This mapping has the property that \(L(x_1,\ldots,x_n)=1\Longleftrightarrow\widetilde L(y_1,\ldots,y_{p(n)})=1\). Furthermore, by the above proof the image decision lists are linearly separable in \(p(n)\)-dimensional space, so \(\operatorname{VCdim}(k\mathrm{DL})=O(p(n))=O(n^k)\). Similar transformation techniques can be found in [KLPV87] and [L88].
